smartlead_campaign: OR #4 | Ecom Leads | Scroll Stopper | NO Personlization

magnet.report_label: Scroll-Stopper Sheet (the 3 ads we wrote them)

thread.subject: made these for you
thread.body_text:
hi Ellie, spent a few minutes on the Twisted Pepper site and I'm a little annoyed we're not working together yet haha.

but that's not why I reached out. I handle Meta ads for ecom brands, and instead of sending a BS pitch, I built you some.

built 3 ad concepts from Twisted Pepper's own products, plus a world-class brand playbook I pulled from your site and customer reviews. it's all done and waiting on my side.

should I send it over?

- Kyle

thread.subject: RE: made these for you
thread.body_text:
Ellie - the thing most brands notice first isn't the ads, it's the brand playbook. it maps your voice, the exact words your customers use in reviews, and what you can and can't say in ad copy.

it's ready to go with the mockups.

 want me to pass both along?

- Kyle
please send me the brand playbook please

here you go Ellie - 3 ad mockups built from Twisted Pepper's own products, plus your brand playbook .

 the mockups show the angles, the playbook is the part most brands actually keep - your voice, the words your customers use, and what you can and can't say in ad copy.

 if any of them click, the quickest way to see how I'd actually run them is a 30 min call. → Calendar 

 - Kyle

copy.tldr: Twisted Pepper is a founder-led gourmet dip, soup and seasoning brand with 103 products but no ads running and no visible reviews, so this call is about starting clean with a bundle-first plan that lifts a low order value, not fixing a broken account.

deep_dive.what_they_are: A handcrafted gourmet food brand started by Ellie Hughes in 2005. They sell dip mixes, soup mixes, BBQ rubs and seasonings direct to consumers online and to wholesale accounts. Built on Shopify.
deep_dive.what_they_sell: Dry mixes you turn into gourmet food at home. Dip mixes, soup mixes, BBQ rubs, seasonings, and gift boxes. Single items run cheap ($5.50 to about $13) and they also sell 3-packs and 4-packs at $22.99 to $36.
deep_dive.positioning: Approachable gourmet. Fine-restaurant flavor made fast and easy for busy home cooks. Gluten free, no MSG, low sodium. Not mass-market bland.
deep_dive.who_buys: Busy home cooks who want bold flavor without the work, and gift-givers buying for people who love to cook or love spice.
customer_voice: I am absolutely addicted to your soups and dips/cheese ball mixes. To be honest - all those I bought from you never went for gifts! They went in our stomachs, and to share with extended family and friends over the holidays.
Jalapeno Lime Rub is absolutely amazing. I use in avacado toast, deviled eggs, steak and chicken.
My husband Loves the Pig Sweat Rub. He sprinkles on his bacon while cooking, and anything pork! I order 4 jars at time.
The Enchilada Sauce Seasoning is so authentic, I don't have to make my Grandmother's Recipe.
strengths: Strong founder story and a clear voice. Ellie is a real person with a real reason for the brand, which makes for good ad hooks.
They already sell multi-packs and gift boxes, and offer free shipping over $40. That gives us a ready-made way to raise the order size, which paid ads need.
gaps: Single products are cheap. Median item is about $9.99, so a one-item sale can barely cover the ad cost. Everything has to push toward the $40 free-shipping threshold.
Almost no reviews show on the site, and the review section still has placeholder text. There is little social proof to lean on in the ads, which makes cold traffic harder to convert.

game_plan.headline: Start from zero with the 3 Scroll-Stopper ads, prove one product can sell profitably at the $40 basket, then widen and add a second hero product.

phases.goal: Prove that cold traffic can buy at a profit at all. Find the one ad and one product that works. Do not chase scale.
campaigns: One simple sales campaign, testing the 3 Scroll-Stopper ads against each other
A basic retargeting campaign for people who visit but do not buy
lead_products: Lead with the Jalapeno Lime Rub / BBQ Rub angle from the Scroll-Stopper first. We have a real, specific customer quote raving about the Jalapeno Lime Rub ('I use in avacado toast, deviled eggs, steak and chicken'), and the homepage is already leaning into 'It's BBQ Time.' Rubs are seasonal-hot right now and the review gives us proof to show. We think rubs/seasonings are the strongest starting point because they are the only products with named fans in the reviews.
Second, push the 4-Pack Dip Mixes ($22.99) as the add-to-cart that gets people to the $40 free-shipping line. This protects the order size.
phases.creative: Run the 3 ads from the Scroll-Stopper Sheet as-is. Start the winner as whichever one leads with the founder story or a single hero product plus the free-shipping offer. Keep the message simple: bold flavor, fast and easy, free shipping over $40.
phases.budget: Start small and hold it steady while we learn.
phases.what_success_looks_like: We can see which ad people click and buy from, and orders are landing near or above the $40 basket. We are not judging profit yet, we are finding the winner.

phases.goal: Put more money behind the winning ad and product. Widen the audience beyond the first small pockets.
campaigns: Scale the winning ad from month one with a bigger, broader audience
Add 2 to 3 fresh ad versions built off the winner's angle
Keep retargeting running and add an offer for repeat buyers
lead_products: Keep pushing whatever won in month one. If it was the rub, keep the rub as the front door and bundle it with a matching seasoning or a 4-pack to hit $40.
phases.creative: Take the angle that won and make more of it. Lean harder on the founder voice and the real customer quotes as we collect more reviews. Add gift-box messaging as the fall gifting season starts to warm up.
phases.budget: Step budget up only on the proven winner. Do not spread it thin across new tests.
phases.what_success_looks_like: The winner keeps returning a profit as we spend more, and the average order is holding at or above $40.

phases.goal: Scale the proven play and add a second hero product so we are not resting on one.
campaigns: Scaled sales campaign on the proven product
New test campaign for a second hero product
Gift-box campaign timed for holiday gifting
lead_products: Add a second front-door product. Best candidate is a Soup Mix (all $9.99) or a Soup Collection 3-pack ($30). The homepage sells 'Soup's On in 30 minutes' and the top review calls the soups something she is 'addicted to.' As the weather cools, soup becomes an easy seasonal pull, and the 3-pack clears the $40 line on its own.
Push Gift Boxes and Gift Bags for the holidays, since they already sell these and gifting is a stated buyer type.
phases.creative: Two lanes now. Lane one is the proven month-one product. Lane two is soup and gift boxes for the cold-weather and holiday season. Use the growing pile of reviews as proof.
phases.budget: Highest of the three months, but weighted toward the proven winner with a smaller slice on the new soup test.
phases.what_success_looks_like: Two products are selling from ads at a profit, and gift-box orders are pulling the average order size up during the holidays.

estimates.label: Starting budget
estimates.basis: enough to get roughly 20 to 40 clicks a day at typical costs for a food brand, which is the floor for learning which of the 3 ads works before we spend more

estimates.label: Average order value we are planning around
estimates.basis: we used the median price of their 103 products as a stand-in, because we cannot see their real Shopify order value. Their free-shipping-over-$40 offer suggests real baskets may be higher, but we will not assume that until Ellie confirms it

estimates.label: Revenue we would aim to add
estimates.value: we are not putting a dollar figure on this yet
estimates.basis: with no ad account access, no real order value, and no current sales data, any revenue number would be a guess. Once Ellie tells us her true average order and margin, we can size a real target. That question is in discovery

assumptions: Real average order value is at or above the $40 free-shipping line, or can be pushed there with bundles. If most orders are single $6 to $10 items, the plan has to lean much harder on packs from day one.
Margins on the mixes are healthy enough to pay for ads on top of a low item price. Dry mixes usually have good margin, but we need Ellie to confirm.
They have never run Meta ads, so we are starting cold with no past data to build on. If they have run ads before, that history changes month one.
The rub/seasoning angle is the strongest start because it is the only product with named fans in the reviews. If Ellie says a different product is her real bestseller, we lead with that instead.

questions.question: Are you running any paid ads right now, on Meta or anywhere else? Have you ever?
questions.listening_for: We could not find any live Meta ads. Confirm they are truly starting from zero. If they ran ads before and stopped, ask why they stopped and what happened.

questions.question: Who handles your marketing today, is it just you or do you have help?
questions.listening_for: Is Ellie doing everything herself? A solo founder means we need to keep this simple and low-effort for her. Also tells us who we would actually work with.

questions.question: When someone buys from you online, what does a typical order come to in dollars?
questions.listening_for: This is the most important number on the call. We used $9.99 as a stand-in. If real orders are near $40, the plan works well. If they are $6 to $10 single items, ads will struggle and we need to talk bundles hard.

questions.question: Roughly what does it cost you to make and ship one of the mixes versus what you sell it for?
questions.listening_for: Whether there is enough margin to pay for ads. Dry mixes usually have room, but we need to hear it. If margin is thin, the whole plan tightens.

questions.question: How much of your sales come from your website versus wholesale and events?
questions.listening_for: If most revenue is wholesale or farmers markets, the online store may be small and ads have less to build on. Tells us how big the DTC opportunity really is.

questions.question: When people land on your site, do many add to cart and then not buy, or do few even get that far?
questions.listening_for: Where it breaks. If people add and drop, it is a checkout or offer problem. If few get far, it is a traffic or product-page problem. Changes what month one focuses on.

questions.question: I noticed your site has very few reviews showing, and some spots still have placeholder text. Do you have reviews collected somewhere else?
questions.listening_for: We have real glowing quotes from the homepage, but the product pages are thin on proof. If she has reviews in email or on Facebook, we can put them to work in ads fast. Social proof is a big lever here.

questions.question: Which product actually sells the most for you today, online or in person?
questions.listening_for: Our plan leads with the rub/seasoning based on the reviews. If her real bestseller is a soup or a dip pack, we switch the lead product. Do not argue, just take the answer.

questions.question: Do you have product photos or short videos of the food made from the mixes, or mostly packaging shots?
questions.listening_for: Food ads live or die on how the food looks. If she only has jar and packet photos, we need finished-dish images before we scale. Affects the creative in month two.

questions.question: Does your free-shipping-over-$40 offer actually get people to spend more, or do most stay under it?
questions.listening_for: If the offer already lifts baskets, great, we build on it. If people order one item and skip it, we need stronger bundle prompts. Directly affects how we protect order size.

questions.question: Do you get people finding you on Google searching for things like gluten free dip mix or BBQ rub, or is it mostly people who already know the brand?
questions.listening_for: These are real search terms people type. Gluten free dip mixes, dry soup mixes and BBQ rubs all get searched. If she is getting some Google traffic already, there is an honest opening for search ads or SEO later. This is a real opening, not a stretch.

questions.question: Do you sell on Amazon or only your own site?
questions.listening_for: Food shoppers search Amazon a lot. If she is not there, that is a separate growth path to note. If she is, ads could support it. Just gathering, not pitching hard.

objections.objection: My products are cheap, a $6 dip mix can't pay for ads.
objections.answer: You are right that a single $6 item can't. That is exactly why we do not sell one item at a time. We build the ads around your 4-packs and your free-shipping-over-$40 offer, so the order lands near $40 not $6. That is the whole point of month one, to prove the basket works before we spend more.

objections.objection: I've never run Facebook or Instagram ads. Isn't that risky?
objections.answer: It is actually cleaner to start fresh than to fix a messy account. We start small, just enough to learn which of the 3 ads I already built for you gets people to buy. We do not scale until something proves it works. You are not betting big on day one.

objections.objection: I don't have many reviews or fancy photos.
objections.answer: You have better proof than you think. Your homepage already has real customers saying they are addicted to your soups and that the Jalapeno Lime Rub is amazing. We put those exact words in the ads. Collecting more reviews and finished-dish photos is part of what we do in the first 60 days, so this gets stronger as we go.

objections.objection: I mostly do wholesale and farmers markets, I'm not sure online ads are worth it.
objections.answer: That is good context, and it means we size this to your real online business, not a fantasy one. If the website is small today, month one is a low-cost test. If it proves out, online sales become a channel you own that runs even when the market is closed.

red_flags.flag: Low item prices against a $40 free-shipping bar.
red_flags.why_it_matters: The median product is about $9.99. If most real orders are single cheap items, the math for paid ads is tough. The plan leans on bundles to fix this, but if baskets stay small, ads may not pay off. Confirm the real order value early.

red_flags.flag: Almost no reviews on the site and placeholder review text still showing.
red_flags.why_it_matters: Cold shoppers need proof before they buy from a brand they do not know. With little visible social proof, ads convert worse. We can use the homepage quotes, but product pages need real reviews to lift results.
If shipping terms are unclear at checkout, people abandon. A missing shipping policy can quietly kill sales from ads. Worth flagging to Ellie so it gets fixed before we send paid traffic.

red_flags.flag: No ad account, Shopify, or analytics access, and no live ads to learn from.
red_flags.why_it_matters: We are starting blind on numbers. Every estimate rests on public data only. Do not state any revenue, spend, or return figure as fact on the call. Get the real order value and margin from Ellie first.

red_flags.flag: 103 products with thin, uneven info and duplicate-looking listings.
red_flags.why_it_matters: A huge catalog with copy-of product URLs and missing details can spread attention thin and confuse shoppers. We should focus ads on a few hero products, not the whole range.

other_channel_openings: Google Search ads: people actively type terms like gluten free dip mix, dry soup mix, and BBQ rub. That is real demand we can catch, so search ads are an honest fit once Meta is working.
SEO on their recipe pages: they already publish recipes like Spinach Artichoke Hot Dip and Gluten Free Pea Soup. Recipe content ranks well in food search and can pull in free traffic over time.
Amazon is worth a question, not a promise: food shoppers search there heavily, so if they are not listed it may be a separate growth path later.
